rationale: The citing paper uses Huang and Zhang (2011) in its core theory section to support a specific premise: in early goal pursuit, higher perceived velocity raises motivation by increasing perceived attainability. That premise is then used to justify how consumers evaluate reserve versus hard and easy goals before adoption, which feeds directly into hypothesis development about preference. Although the 2017 paper does not reuse the velocity manipulation or test the cited paper directly, the citation plays a meaningful conceptual framing role rather than a passing literature-list mention.
relation_description: The citing paper draws on Huang and Zhang (2011) as prior evidence that early-stage goal motivation is sensitive to perceived velocity and attainability cues. It uses this result as part of the conceptual foundation for its own account of why reserve goals should be attractive at signup.
